topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished oak of the office gleamed under the soft lighting, a stark contrast to the dusty, cramped hallways of middle school where Mark had held court. Sarah watched him from across the reception desk, a tight knot tightening in her stomach. He was fielding calls, his voice calm, measured, utterly devoid of the sneering mockery she remembered so vividly. He was *helping* people now, offering solace where he used to dispense cruelty. She traced the rim of her lukewarm coffee cup, the ceramic suddenly feeling cold and inadequate. Every successful session he navigated felt like a direct refutation of her own perceived failures. When he smiled—a genuine, warm curve of the lips directed at a nervous client—a sharp, unpleasant pressure built behind her eyes. She gripped her handbag strap until her knuckles turned white, wanting to shatter something, anything, just to disrupt the perfect, serene tableau he had constructed.

[story 2]

"Another breakthrough," Mark murmured, leaning back in his leather chair, the pen hovering over his notepad. His tone was soothing, the kind of gentle authority that used to intimidate her into silence. Across the room, Chloe felt a sudden, intense heat rise in her chest, a restless churning that made her shift uncomfortably on the sofa. She watched him interact with another patient, his posture radiating an effortless competence. It wasn't just that he was successful; it was that he possessed this quiet, earned acceptance that she had spent years desperately chasing through antagonism. A bitter taste coated her tongue. She picked at a loose thread on the cushion, her fingers twitching with an urge to lash out, to introduce some jarring discord into the peaceful atmosphere. She needed to prove that his sanctuary was fragile, that his composure was a carefully constructed façade about to crack under the weight of her own unacknowledged yearning.

[story 3]

I stood by the window, the city lights blurring through the condensation on the glass. He was on the phone, his voice low, explaining a complex emotional dynamic to someone. The sound of his voice—so measured, so empathetic—felt like a physical slap. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. It was infuriating. He had taken the very energy I once expended in tormenting others and repurposed it into something undeniably valuable. Every time he spoke of empathy, I felt a sharp, internal recoil. I walked over to the bookshelf, my hand hovering over a stack of novels, my fingers flexing. I wanted to scream that he was fundamentally changed, that his newfound kindness was a sham, a calculated performance designed to mask the same sharp edges that had once defined him. The silence in the room amplified the frantic rhythm of my pulse.
